speaker: Ivy Reid Lewis and Charles Rogers Reid
institution: African American Museum and Library at Oakland
rights: Copyright status unknown. Contact AAMLO for permissions.
text: I was born in Angels Camp, Calvary County, September 22, 1898, and my family came to Berkeley in 1903, which Sam Papal Avenue was a dirt road in University Avenue was a dirt road. I'm one of 13 children next to the oldest. I can remember the big earthquake and fire in San Francisco April 18, 1906. And as a child, I like to play baseball, and I can remember when I had my father's tick annum for a bat. And my mother took a rubber ball and put string around it and made a ball for him. And I got to be a good ball player. I played on the first baseball team at San Papal Park, 1914, and I attended Franklin Elementary School and Richmond High School. And I got to be a good ball player. I played with a pitch for the Pierce Giants, one of the best black teams around the Bay. And I pitched in Richmond at 1st and McDonald, 1921, 2223, and 24. I came to Richmond in 1935, and that was at Nickel Park, and they had a big game between the Aldro Company and Richmond Lertens, and they didn't have any umpire. And I was up in the stands as somebody recognized me and they asked me to umpire. And I umpired the game, the game won 12 innings. And they liked the way I umpired and they thought we were protecting the mask and the shinglass. I started umpiring all the games at Nickel Park. Two hardball games on Sunday, three games a night on the industrial league, the Stanagolo League in the city. And I met George Miller. His son is a big consul, big congressman now. He used to do the pitching. And Greg was a judge, he was a catcher. And Vessel and Bear played Major Hill and Shared Flown. And when this job came up on the playground in 1947, they recommended me for it. And I went to work at the park, an off-richment park in February 1947. And I put up a good basketball court and then I had a very pitch horse shoes. And I started all the black boys and girls participating in sports. I knew the advantage of a black boy in sports. And in 1949, his full boys from North Richmond played on the high school team coach for Mr. Christensen. Jim McKellar, Carl Lawson, James Brown and you McKelvie, a won the championship. And it came out there after they won the championship and fixed up a hard surface basketball court and put up the lights. And I also had two baseball teams I managed while I was in Richmond. The spiders and the clean-up torn cleaners. And I was 50 years of age but I could still throw that ball. And in 1951, Carl Lawson organized a Twilight League and we started playing every night on the basketball court. And the first time I had Casey Jones, Bill Russell, Mike Farmer, Paul Silas, Jim Hadnott, Bade Williams, Tyler Wilben, JD Banks, Clyde Hardeman, Lisa Dixon. James Moore, Willie Preston, Miller-Jellis, James Preston, John Preston, Lawrence Moore, Lorenzo Moore, Leroy Walker, John Walker, Nat Dates, a mayor now he was playing, Willie Reed and Consumin Fritz Adams playing and Bob Campbell. And I had the Twilight League from 1951 to 1966 and I didn't allow any boy or girl to smoke cigarettes on the playground. I seen 500 boys and girls on the playground not a one-on-smoke to cigarette. And if they were smoking and seeing me coming, they would hide it from me. And I used to give them balls and bats and kick balls on holidays and Sundays and he always bought them back. And I worked until September 1968, that's when I retired. And they dedicated the new building after I retired and they put my name on them. And I also, the name the basketball league at the Richmond Auditorium during the month of December, they named that after me. And I hope to stay in Richmond until I die. I've been here since 1935. That's all, thank you.

segments.text: I was born in Angels Camp, Calvary County, September 22, 1898, and my family came to Berkeley
in 1903, which Sam Papal Avenue was a dirt road in University Avenue was a dirt road.
I'm one of 13 children next to the oldest.
I can remember the big earthquake and fire in San Francisco April 18, 1906.
And as a child, I like to play baseball, and I can remember when I had my father's
tick annum for a bat.
And my mother took a rubber ball and put string around it and made a ball for him.
And I got to be a good ball player.
I played on the first baseball team at San Papal Park, 1914, and I attended Franklin Elementary
School and Richmond High School.
And I got to be a good ball player.
I played with a pitch for the Pierce Giants, one of the best black teams around the Bay.
And I pitched in Richmond at 1st and McDonald, 1921, 2223, and 24.
I came to Richmond in 1935, and that was at Nickel Park, and they had a big game between
the Aldro Company and Richmond Lertens, and they didn't have any umpire.
And I was up in the stands as somebody recognized me and they asked me to umpire.
And I umpired the game, the game won 12 innings.
And they liked the way I umpired and they thought we were protecting the mask and the
shinglass.
I started umpiring all the games at Nickel Park.
Two hardball games on Sunday, three games a night on the industrial league, the Stanagolo
League in the city.
And I met George Miller.
His son is a big consul, big congressman now.
He used to do the pitching.
And Greg was a judge, he was a catcher.
And Vessel and Bear played Major Hill and Shared Flown.
And when this job came up on the playground in 1947, they recommended me for it.
And I went to work at the park, an off-richment park in February 1947.
And I put up a good basketball court and then I had a very pitch horse shoes.
And I started all the black boys and girls participating in sports.
I knew the advantage of a black boy in sports.
And in 1949, his full boys from North Richmond played on the high school team coach for
Mr. Christensen.
Jim McKellar, Carl Lawson, James Brown and you McKelvie, a won the championship.
And it came out there after they won the championship and fixed up a hard surface basketball court
and put up the lights.
And I also had two baseball teams I managed while I was in Richmond.
The spiders and the clean-up torn cleaners.
And I was 50 years of age but I could still throw that ball.
And in 1951, Carl Lawson organized a Twilight League and we started playing every night on the basketball court.
And the first time I had Casey Jones, Bill Russell, Mike Farmer, Paul Silas, Jim Hadnott,
Bade Williams, Tyler Wilben, JD Banks, Clyde Hardeman, Lisa Dixon.
James Moore, Willie Preston, Miller-Jellis, James Preston,
John Preston, Lawrence Moore, Lorenzo Moore, Leroy Walker, John Walker, Nat Dates,
a mayor now he was playing, Willie Reed and Consumin Fritz Adams playing and Bob Campbell.
And I had the Twilight League from 1951 to 1966 and I didn't allow any boy or girl to smoke cigarettes on the playground.
I seen 500 boys and girls on the playground not a one-on-smoke to cigarette.
And if they were smoking and seeing me coming, they would hide it from me.
And I used to give them balls and bats and kick balls on holidays and Sundays and he always bought them back.
And I worked until September 1968, that's when I retired.
And they dedicated the new building after I retired and they put my name on them.
And I also, the name the basketball league at the Richmond Auditorium during the month of December, they named that after me.
And I hope to stay in Richmond until I die. I've been here since 1935. That's all, thank you.
